key_insight: Foundation ML interatomic potentials can reproduce some low-temperature ordering in correlated perovskite oxides, but their success depends strongly on whether the order is scalar, vector, or purely on-site in character.
engineering_relevance: For computational materials design, this work shows where foundation potentials are reliable replacements for DFT in oxide structure/dynamics and where correlated-electron physics still requires caution or higher-fidelity methods.
method: Tested three foundation MLIPs on LaMnO3, LaCoO3, and NdNiO3 without system-specific training.
Ran 1 ns molecular dynamics on 80- and 160-atom supercells across 50–300 K.
Compared whether each model recovered the expected low-temperature bond, orbital, and spin order.
evidence_signal: The evaluation spans three isostructural ABO3 oxides with distinct order types, and the abstract reports that NdNiO3 is captured, LaMnO3 is partially captured, and LaCoO3 remains inaccessible to current models.
watch_item: These results indicate that foundation MLIPs may miss symmetry-specific or purely local spin-state transitions, so validation against correlated-electron order is essential before deployment.
paper_title: Bond, orbital and spin order in d4/d6/d7 perovskite oxides: successes and limitations of foundation interatomic potentials
